rationale_for_baseline_tier: Skin-limited acute urticaria in a haemodynamically stable adult with a NEGATIVE airway/respiratory/circulatory screen is a low-acuity, telehealth/GP-manageable problem — NOT an emergency. It rates T2 (prompt, same-day-to-48h telehealth or GP contact) rather than T0/T1 because a standard-dose antihistamine has already failed and the patient needs prescriber-guided up-dosing and consideration of a short steroid course, plus firm conditional safety-netting. It is explicitly NOT T4/T5: the note's in-person after-hours 'imminent anaphylaxis' framing is MBS-billing language, not the patient's true acuity, and escalating this stable presentation to ED/000 would be an over-triage. The single non-negotiable is conditional escalation: if airway/anaphylaxis features appear, it becomes T5 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Swelling of the lips, tongue or throat; voice change; difficulty breathing or swallowing; wheeze; or feeling faint/dizzy
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately, within minutes — do not wait
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of angioedema/anaphylaxis — an immediate threat to the airway and circulation. Adrenaline (if an autoinjector is available) and an emergency ambulance are time-critical.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your lips, tongue or throat start to swell, your voice changes, you have any trouble breathing or swallowing, or you feel faint — call 000 immediately, say it may be a severe allergic reaction, and lie down. If you have an adrenaline autoinjector, use it into the outer thigh.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Individual weals lasting longer than 24 hours, becoming painful or burning, or leaving bruising/pigmentation; or the rash not settling despite up-dosed antihistamines over a few days
escalation_edges.rationale: Persistent, painful or bruising lesions suggest urticarial vasculitis rather than ordinary urticaria and warrant GP review and possible investigation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If any single welt hangs around for more than a day, becomes painful rather than itchy, or leaves a bruise, or if the rash isn't settling on the higher-dose antihistamines after a few days, see your GP promptly so it can be looked into further.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New fever, joint pains, or feeling systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Systemic symptoms move the picture beyond simple urticaria and warrant reassessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a fever, joint aches, or start to feel generally unwell, contact your GP or telehealth service the same day for a review.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Independent adult living alone with no one else at home tonight
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Because she is alone, stress that if any airway swelling or breathing difficulty develops she must call 000 immediately rather than wait to see if it passes, keep her phone within reach, and unlock the front door for paramedics. Advise her to tell a friend or neighbour she is unwell so someone can check on her.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This looks like an acute hives reaction that is usually self-limiting and can be managed at home. Take a regular higher dose of a non-sedating antihistamine as directed, use cool compresses, and avoid hot showers, tight clothing and hard exercise. It should settle over the coming days, though new welts may keep appearing for a while.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Telehealth re-contact or your regular GP; call 000 for any airway or breathing warning signs
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If warning signs of a severe allergic reaction appear after hours, call 000 immediately — do not wait for clinic hours. For non-urgent worsening, an after-hours GP service or telehealth line is appropriate.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide a note of the acute urticaria episode, the antihistamine up-dosing and any short steroid course, and the advice to consider allergy referral only if it becomes chronic (>6 weeks) or recurrent.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Dismissing the rash as trivial and closing the encounter with no airway/anaphylaxis safety-netting and no 000 threshold.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: If the reaction later progresses to angioedema/anaphylaxis, a patient who was reassured with no warning signs may not recognise the emergency and delay a life-saving 000 call, risking airway obstruction.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Treating a stable, skin-limited urticaria as anaphylaxis and sending the patient to the ED or calling a 000 ambulance despite a negative red-flag screen.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether this acute urticaria will remain skin-limited or progress to angioedema/anaphylaxis, which cannot be predicted with certainty at first contact.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat as benign acute urticaria (the current, evidence-based read) AND give explicit, specific airway/anaphylaxis safety-netting with an immediate 000 threshold — reassurance and a clear escalation plan together.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either extreme: dismissing the patient without any safety-net, OR over-escalating a stable patient to emergency care / adrenaline because progression is theoretically possible.
